title: Remote Work Productivity (MCP semantic-referee smoke test)
resolution: Software engineering productivity drops by at least twenty percent when companies require full remote work, because the loss of in-person collaboration creates communication overhead that no async tooling can fully replace, and the productivity gains from eliminated commutes do not offset this loss.

moves.body: Software engineering productivity drops by at least twenty percent under full remote-work mandates. The mechanism is concrete: shared physical context — the whiteboard sketch, the impromptu hallway question, the back-of-envelope walkthrough — produces dense, high-bandwidth information transfer that asynchronous tooling cannot match. Slack threads, recorded walkthroughs, and shared docs are useful, but they are bandwidth-limited substitutes. The commute time recovered under remote work is a real personal gain, but it does not offset the team-level communication overhead that emerges when complex multi-engineer coordination must route through async channels. The twenty percent figure represents this gap.

You stated 'at least twenty percent' as if it were a settled finding, but the move provides no primary source for the figure. Which study? Which methodology? Which population was measured? Without an actual citation — paper title, authors, year, and the specific table or sentence the figure comes from — the number is unfalsifiable. A productivity drop of that magnitude across all of software engineering would be a major empirical claim. It needs a primary source, not just the assertion.

displayMeta.quoteAnchorCandidate: no primary source for the figure

moves.body: The reference is a Stanford working paper from 2022 by Bloom and colleagues on the effects of remote-work mandates on engineering productivity. The paper is part of Stanford's working paper series and supports the directional claim — remote-work mandates correlate with productivity decline in coordination-heavy roles. The specific supporting sentence is in the paper; I'll surface it in a follow-up if needed.

evidence.label: Stanford working paper (2022, Bloom et al.) on remote-work productivity
evidence.sourceText: Stanford working paper series, 2022, by Bloom and colleagues on remote-work productivity. The paper documents directional correlations between full remote-work mandates and engineering productivity decline in coordination-heavy roles. The specific table, page reference, and supporting sentence are not surfaced in this move — see follow-up.

moves.body: Setting aside the missing primary citation for a moment: which productivity metric does the twenty percent figure measure? Lines of code committed per week? Features shipped per quarter? Code review turnaround time? Time-to-resolution on production bugs? Velocity points completed? Each of these is a defensible operational definition, and they do not move together. A twenty percent drop in commit volume looks very different from a twenty percent drop in customer-facing impact. Without the definition pinned down, the figure is rhetorical, not empirical.

Even setting the specific figure aside, the broader pattern is widely observed. Engineering leaders at major firms — across companies of different sizes, different stacks, different industries — have publicly stated that productivity declined under their remote-work mandates. The consensus across that group is itself a signal. When that many senior engineering leaders independently report the same direction of effect, the burden shifts to explaining why their observations should be discounted.

displayMeta.quoteAnchorCandidate: consensus across that group is itself a signal

moves.targetExcerpt: consensus across that group is itself a signal
moves.body: The number of senior leaders who hold an opinion does not make that opinion evidence. Many leaders held strong opinions about waterfall over agile, COBOL over Java, mainframe over distributed — and on several of those, the consensus turned out to be wrong. Popularity within a professional cohort is not the same as measurable productivity data. I'll renew the earlier request: please surface the specific sentence from the Stanford 2022 paper that supports the twenty percent figure. The paper either contains that sentence or it does not.

Fair pushback. The 'at least twenty percent' figure may not be defensible as a universal claim across the full population of software engineering work — there are roles and team configurations where the effect is small or negligible. I'll narrow what I'm defending: for complex multi-team coordination work — large feature programs that require sustained alignment across multiple engineering organizations and product surfaces — the productivity drop under fully remote operation is real and observable, even if the precise figure is contested. The narrower claim is the one I want to stake.

Accepted: the narrower claim about multi-team coordination work is more defensible than the universal version, and is a real empirical question worth investigating on its own terms. Both sides agree the original 'at least twenty percent across all software engineering' framing was too strong. The narrower version — productivity friction in complex multi-team coordination work under fully remote operation — survives. I'd recommend branching off here: the open question is whether sufficient investment in async coordination infrastructure (richer documentation standards, structured design reviews, persistent recorded walkthroughs) could close the narrower gap. That's a separate empirical question with its own evidence base.

notes: Eight-move smoke-test scenario for the MCP semantic-referee. Walks an argument arc from opening claim through evidence challenge, vague-citation evasion, definition challenge, popularity appeal, anti-amplification challenge, narrowing concession, to synthesis with branch recommendation. Predicted classifier signals use the real MCP-011 23-id catalog vocabulary (option B substitutions per docs/testing-runs/2026-05-22-smoke-test-verification-scans.md). Exercises 11 distinct catalog ids positively across the eight moves (responds_to_parent, introduces_new_issue, asks_for_evidence, provides_evidence, creates_source_chain_gap, requests_clarification, uses_popularity_as_evidence, narrows_claim, concedes_narrow_point, ready_for_synthesis, suggests_side_branch) and explicitly predicts negative signals where it matters (responds_to_parent=0 on the root, provides_evidence=0 on the assertion-only and popularity-appeal moves, evidence_supports_claim=0 on the vague-citation move, quote_anchors_parent=0 on the unquoted-citation move, asks_for_evidence=0 on the definition challenge, creates_source_chain_gap=0 on the Revocateur's source request). The anti-amplification exchange (moves 5 and 6) is represented in the catalog as the speaker-side uses_popularity_as_evidence=1 paired with the challenger-side asks_for_evidence=1 + requests_clarification=1 — the catalog has no paired challenger classifier; this is a finding rather than a gap to fix.
